---
title: "One Piece Data Science Project"
author: "Jezel Valdez, John Esparragoza, Shelby Townnsend"
date: "May 3rd, 2026"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    code_folding: hide
    theme: flatly
    number_sections: true
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  results = "hide",
  fig.align = "center"
)

library(tidyverse)
library(jsonlite)
library(lubridate)
library(knitr)

set.seed(518)
```

# Introduction

## Project Motivation

*One Piece* is one of the most popular and longest-running anime and manga series ever created. The world of *One Piece* contains hundreds of characters, pirate crews, islands, episodes, and supernatural powers, making it perfect for data analysis.

The purpose of this project is to explore patterns within the *One Piece* universe using data science techniques learned throughout the semester. In particular, we investigate relationships between pirate crews, character bounties, devil fruits, islands, and episode releases.

We really wanted to explore the world of One Piece with this data rather than just analysis because not everybody is familiar with the material. We begin by cleaning and preparing the datasets, then move into exploratory analysis to identify trends and relationships, before concluding with the major findings and limitations of the project.

---

# Data & Methods

## Data Sources

The datasets used in this project came from the following API:

- https://api-onepiece.com/en/documentation

We collected data from multiple endpoints, including:
- Characters
- Crews
- Devil Fruits
- Episodes
- Locations

The API provided rich information but required substantial cleaning before analysis.

---

## Importing the Data

The code below imports all datasets directly from the API. Using API calls instead of manually downloaded files improves reproducibility because the report can automatically refresh whenever the data updates.

```{r}
# Character data
url1 <- "https://api.api-onepiece.com/v2/characters/en"
characters <- fromJSON(url1)
characters_df <- as.data.frame(characters)

# Location data
url2 <- "https://api.api-onepiece.com/v2/locates/en"
locations <- fromJSON(url2)
locations_df <- as.data.frame(locations)

# Episode data
url3 <- "https://api.api-onepiece.com/v2/episodes/en"
episodes <- fromJSON(url3)
episodes_df <- as.data.frame(episodes)

# Crew data
url4 <- "https://api.api-onepiece.com/v2/crews/en"
crews <- fromJSON(url4)
crews_df <- as.data.frame(crews)

# Devil fruit data
url5 <- "https://api.api-onepiece.com/v2/fruits/en"
fruits <- fromJSON(url5)
fruits_df <- as.data.frame(fruits)
```

---

# Data Cleaning

## Cleaning Character Data

The original datasets contained missing values, numbers stored as text, inconsistent formatting, and parts of the data in French.

Before analysis, we converted important variables into usable numeric formats.

```{r cleaning}
characters_df <- characters_df %>%
  mutate(
    bounty = as.numeric(str_remove_all(bounty, "\\.")),
    age = as.numeric(str_remove_all(age, "ans")),
    size = as.numeric(str_remove_all(size, "cm"))
  )
```


Data preparation note: numeric variables such as bounty, age, and size originally appeared as character strings. These were converted into numeric values so they could be analyzed statistically.

---

## Joining Crew Data

To compare characters across pirate crews, we merged the crew dataset into the character dataset.

```{r crew-join}
crew_full_df <- as.data.frame(characters_df$crew)

# converting data types
crew_full_df <- crew_full_df %>%
  mutate(
    total_prime = as.numeric(str_remove_all(total_prime, "\\.")),
    number = as.numeric(str_remove_all(number, "\\."))
  ) 

# removed the crew & joined the actual crew df
characters_with_crews <- bind_cols(
  characters_df %>% select(-crew),
  crew_full_df %>% rename(
    crew_id = id,
    crew_name = name,
    crew_status = status,
    crew_number = number,
    crew_total_prime = total_prime)) %>% 
    select(-description, -roman_name)
```

The merged dataset allows us to analyze how crew membership relates to bounty values, occupations, and other character traits.

We also converted some of the language from French to English, as well as joining the devil fruit data with the rest of the data frame.
```{r}
dev_fruit_df <- as.data.frame(characters_with_crews$fruit) %>%
  select(-roman_name, -filename, -technicalFile)   # removing unwanted columns

# merging table
full_character_df <- bind_cols(
  characters_with_crews %>% select(-fruit),
  dev_fruit_df %>%
    rename(devil_fruit = name) %>%
    select(-id)
)

full_character_df

characters_with_crews <- characters_with_crews %>%
  mutate(
    crew_name = case_when(
      crew_name == "The Chapeau de Paille crew" ~ "Straw Hat Pirates",
      crew_name == "Armarda du Chapeau de Paille" ~ "Straw Hat Grand Fleet",
      crew_name == "Baggy's Delivery" ~ "Buggy's Delivery",
      crew_name == "Le Roux crew" ~ "Red Hair Pirates",
      TRUE ~ crew_name
    )
  )
```
---

# Exploratory Data Analysis

## Distribution of Character Bounties

Character bounties are one of the most important measures of power and danger in the *One Piece* world.

The histogram below shows the distribution of bounty values across characters.

```{r bounty-distribution, fig.cap="Character bounty values are heavily right-skewed because only a small number of characters possess extremely large bounties."}
characters_df %>%
  filter(!is.na(bounty)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = bounty)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "blue") +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Character Bounties",
    x = "Bounty",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

The distribution is strongly right-skewed. Most characters have relatively low or moderate bounties, while a small number of elite pirates possess extremely high bounty values.

This skewness suggests that using only the mean could be misleading, so median values are also important when comparing groups.

---

# Characters With and Without Bounties

Not every character in the series is a pirate or criminal. The figure below compares how many characters have bounty values versus those without one.

```{r}
characters_df %>%
  mutate(has_bounty = if_else(is.na(bounty), "No", "Yes")) %>%
  count(has_bounty) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = has_bounty, y = n, fill = has_bounty)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(
    title = "Characters With and Without Bounties",
    x = "Has Bounty",
    y = "Character Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

The results show that many characters do not possess bounties. This makes sense because the dataset includes civilians, government members, and side characters in addition to pirates.

---

# Inline Code Example

The cleaned dataset contains `r nrow(characters_df)` total characters.

Approximately `r round(mean(!is.na(characters_df$bounty)) * 100, 1)`% of characters possess a bounty.

Using inline code ensures that values update automatically whenever the dataset changes.

---

# Relationships Between Variables

## Captains vs Fighters

Next, we explored whether captains tend to have larger bounties than standard fighters.

```{r captain-vs-fighter}
captain_fighter_summary <- characters_with_crews %>%
  filter(job %in% c("Captain", "Fighter")) %>%
  group_by(job) %>%
  summarise(
    Average_Bounty = round(mean(bounty, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    Median_Bounty = round(median(bounty, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    Count = n()
  )

kable(
  captain_fighter_summary,
  caption = "Captains generally possess larger bounty values than fighters."
)
```

Captains have substantially higher average bounty values than fighters. The median bounty is also higher for captains, which confirms that the pattern is not driven entirely by extreme outliers.

---

#Devil Fruit Analysis
```{r}

```

Although many characters do not possess devil fruits, fruit users are heavily represented among major pirates and important antagonists.

---

# Island Analysis

The *One Piece* world contains many different oceans and regions. We compared the number of islands in each major sea.

```{r islands, fig.cap="The Grand Line and major seas contain significantly different numbers of islands, highlighting the scale of the One Piece world."}
locations_df %>%
  count(sea_name) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(sea_name, n), y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "darkorange") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Number of Islands by Region",
    x = "Region",
    y = "Island Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

Some regions contain substantially more islands than others, especially the Grand Line, which serves as the primary setting for much of the series.

---

# Episode Analysis

## Episodes Released Per Decade

The *One Piece* anime has aired continuously for decades. We analyzed how episode releases changed over time.

```{r episode-decade, fig.cap="Episode production has remained consistently high across multiple decades of the anime's run."}
episodes_df <- episodes_df %>%
  mutate(
    year = year(as.Date(release_date)),
    decade = floor(year / 10) * 10
  )

episodes_df %>%
  count(decade) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = factor(decade), y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "purple") +
  labs(
    title = "Episodes Released Per Decade",
    x = "Decade",
    y = "Episodes"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

The visualization demonstrates the remarkable longevity of the series. Few television franchises maintain such consistent production over multiple decades.

---

# Visualization Best Practices

Throughout this report, we followed several visualization best practices:

- Every graph includes clear axis labels
- Each figure has a caption explaining the main takeaway
- Colors were chosen for readability
- Minimal themes reduce unnecessary clutter
- Graph types match the structure of the data

For example:
- Histograms were used for distributions
- Bar charts were used for category comparisons
- Tables were used when exact values mattered

---

# Tables

Tables are useful when readers need exact values rather than general patterns.

```{r summary-table}
summary_table <- characters_df %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Characters = n(),
    Characters_With_Bounties = sum(!is.na(bounty)),
    Average_Bounty = round(mean(bounty, na.rm = TRUE), 2)
  )

kable(
  summary_table,
  caption = "Summary statistics for the cleaned character dataset."
)
```

Unlike raw data frame output, formatted tables improve readability and make important values easier to interpret.

---

# Common Pitfalls

Several common issues appeared during this project:

- Missing values
- Inconsistent variable formats
- Extreme outliers
- Duplicate observations
- API fields written in French

One important lesson from this project is that code working in the console does not always mean it will work when knitting the report. Because RMarkdown runs in a fresh session, every required object and package must be created within the document itself.

We also avoided:
- printing raw data frames,
- using plots without interpretation,
- and manually typing numeric results into prose.

---

# Results & Discussion

The analysis revealed several important patterns:

- Character bounties are highly skewed
- Captains consistently possess larger bounties than fighters
- Devil fruit users are strongly associated with major characters
- The *One Piece* world contains substantial regional variation in island counts
- The anime has maintained long-term production consistency over decades

These findings reflect how the *One Piece* universe is structured narratively. Powerful characters and major pirate crews dominate the story, while geography and long-running arcs contribute to the scale of the series.

---

# Conclusion

This project demonstrated how data science techniques can be applied to entertainment media and fictional universes.

Using *One Piece* datasets, we:
- cleaned messy API data,
- merged multiple datasets,
- explored distributions and relationships,
- created visualizations and tables,
- and practiced reproducible reporting techniques in RMarkdown.

One limitation of the project is that some API fields were incomplete or inconsistent. Additionally, bounty values are highly skewed, which can distort averages.

Despite these limitations, the project successfully showed how exploratory data analysis and storytelling can transform raw data into meaningful insights.

---
